nodes.body: The tempfile cleanup uses asyncio.shield(asyncio.to_thread(...)) but the returned awaitable is never awaited or scheduled, so the unlink may never run and can also trigger "coroutine was never awaited" warnings. Use a background task (e.g., asyncio.create_task(asyncio.to_thread(...))) or await the shielded call where appropriate.

Because the backend servers are started in the background, `set -e` won't stop the script if one of these processes fails immediately; the proxy will still start and then return 502s. Consider adding a simple readiness/health check loop (or at least verifying the PIDs are still alive after a short delay) before starting the proxy, and exit non-zero if a backend didn't come up.

Setting pytest-cov to --cov-fail-under=0 effectively disables coverage enforcement in pre-commit, which makes it much easier for regressions to land unnoticed (especially since API tests were removed in this PR). Restore an appropriate minimum threshold or gate only the affected packages/tests if full coverage is temporarily infeasible.

The module-level httpx.AsyncClient is closed in the app lifespan, which can break scenarios where the app is started/stopped multiple times in the same process (e.g., TestClient contexts or reload) because subsequent requests will use a closed client. Create the AsyncClient inside lifespan startup (or store it on app.state) and reference that per-lifespan instance in proxy_request, closing it on shutdown.

proxy_request builds the upstream URL using request.url.path only, so any query string (e.g., ?stream=true, pagination params, etc.) will be dropped when forwarding. Include request.url.query (or use request.url.components to reconstruct the full URL) so query parameters are preserved.

The upstream httpx Response returned by client.send(..., stream=True) is never explicitly closed. With StreamingResponse(response.aiter_raw(), ...) this can leak connections/file descriptors under load. Wrap the body iterator in a generator with a try/finally that awaits response.aclose() (or use client.stream(...) context manager) and consider stripping hop-by-hop headers (e.g., connection/transfer-encoding/content-length) before returning them.
